title: Ngā Mahi a te Whānau o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / The Practices of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream Family
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the history of Ngāti Kahungunu and the Mangakōtukutuku stream that flows through our rohe. Students will explore how their tūpuna used traditional methods like hīnaki (eel traps), pā tuna (eel weirs), and rongoā (medicinal plants) from the awa, connecting language learning to the very waters that sustain our community.
learningObjectives: Identify and name at least 5 traditional tools and practices (e.g., hīnaki, pā tuna, rongoā) used at Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using correct te reo Māori terms
Construct simple sentences in te reo Māori describing how these traditional methods were used and their connection to the environment
Participate in a waiata or karakia related to the Mangakōtukutuku stream, demonstrating understanding of key vocabulary
Create a visual representation (digital or physical) of a traditional practice with te reo Māori labels and descriptions

activities.title: He Hīkoi ki te Awa / Walk to the Stream
activities.description: Students will take a guided hīkoi to the Mangakōtukutuku stream with a kaumātua. They will learn the Māori names for native plants (e.g., harakeke, kōwhai) and observe potential historical sites for traditional practices while listening to stories of the awa.

activities.title: Ngā Taputapu o nehe / Tools of the Past
activities.description: In the classroom, students will handle replicas (or images) of traditional tools like a hīnaki (eel trap). They will learn the te reo Māori names for each part and practice sentences describing their use: 'He aha tēnei? He hīnaki tēnei. Hei hopu tuna.'

activities.title: Te Mahi Rongoā / The Medicine Work
activities.description: Focusing on rongoā Māori, students will learn the names and properties of specific plants found near our awa (e.g., kawakawa for stomach aches). They will create simple flashcards with the plant name, its use, and a sentence: 'He rongoā te kawakawa mō te mamae puku.'

activities.title: Te Waiata o te Awa / The Song of the Stream
activities.description: Students will learn a waiata or karakia that speaks of or is dedicated to a waterway, such as 'E rere e te wai.' They will break down the lyrics, translate key phrases, and perform it, understanding its cultural significance to the Mangakōtukutuku stream.

activities.title: He Whakaaturanga mō te Whānau / A Presentation for the Whānau
activities.description: In small groups, students will create a short digital slideshow or poster. They will choose one traditional practice, describe it in te reo Māori using learned vocabulary and sentence structures, and explain its importance to Ngāti Kahungunu and our local environment.

resources: Kaumātua from local Ngāti Kahungunu marae (e.g., representatives from Omahu Marae) to share mātauranga
Replica traditional Māori tools (e.g., hīnaki, hue) from the college's cultural resource kit
The book 'Ngā Rongoā o te Ngahere' by Rob McGowan or similar resources on rongoā
Te Aka Māori Dictionary online (maoridictionary.co.nz) for vocabulary reinforcement

tasks: Oral language check: Students correctly identify and describe 5 tools/practices using te reo Māori
Completion of flashcards with correct te reo Māori terms and simple sentences
Active participation in learning and performing the waiata/karakia
Group presentation demonstrating use of target vocabulary and cultural understanding

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 4: Use relevant pronunciation, greetings, and vocabulary to communicate about daily activities and events
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Level 4: Understand that Māori language is a living language
Social Sciences: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
The Arts: Developing Practical Knowledge - Level 4: Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and selected principles
